post: I live with my parents, who have no idea how bad my depression is. They think my room is messy because I'm lazy, but in reality, I just don't have the strength to clean it. I also suffer with sensory issues which makes cleaning harder. My room isn't even messy anymore; it's filthy. Being a 32yo girls girl, I mostly struggle with managing my bin, which has resulted in a rat infestation in my room. My parents don't know and I'm scared to tell them in case they get mad. But I don't know how to fix it on my own, especially since I don't do well with things like that. Unfortunately, I need to get my room clean in a week and I just don't know what to do. I've always struggled with cleaning but my depression has made everything so much harder, so I'm very worried that it won't get done in time. Any advice would be greatly appreciated. Thank you. Update: Thank you so much for all of your advice. I’m happy to report that although my room isn’t spotless, it’s clean and liveable. Thank you for all of your advice and kind words.
gpt4_response:
First, I want to say how much strength you’ve shown by reaching out and sharing what you’re experiencing. Depression can make the simplest tasks feel completely overwhelming, and adding sensory challenges on top makes it even tougher—this isn’t a reflection of your character or ability, but of how much you’ve been carrying lately.

It’s understandable to be nervous about telling your parents. Many people in your situation feel worried about potential reactions or fear being misunderstood. Please remember your health and safety are important, and everyone deserves support and compassion, especially when things feel overwhelming.

When dealing with a task that feels monumental, it can help to break it down into the smallest possible steps. For example:
- **Start small.** Set a timer for 5–10 minutes and focus on just one area (for example, collecting all the trash into one bag).
- **Use tools that make things easier.** Wear gloves or a mask if smells or textures are triggering. Sometimes listening to music or a favorite podcast can provide helpful distraction.
- **Give yourself permission to pause.** If you start feeling overwhelmed, step away for a few minutes and practice some deep breathing—just getting started is already a win.
- **Consider outside help if possible.** Is there a friend or trusted person you could invite over just for moral support or to keep you company while you work? Sometimes just not being alone with the task can really help.
- **After cleaning, keep your room as inviting as possible for yourself.** Little self-care touches—a comforting blanket, a scented candle—can make your space more soothing.

If telling your parents feels necessary (especially since rodent issues can affect the whole household), you might try writing down what you want to say, or even writing a note if talking feels too hard. Let them know you’re struggling and that you’re doing your best to fix things. Most people are more understanding than we fear once they know the whole picture.

Remember, you aren’t alone. Your struggles are valid, and you deserve kindness, both from others and from yourself. If cleaning feels unmanageable on your own, sometimes professional cleaning services can be helpful, and there’s absolutely no shame in seeking that help.

Above all, take it one step at a time, and take care of yourself in the process. You’ve already shown a lot of courage by facing this and reaching out.
rationale: The response is highly warm, supportive, and nonjudgmental, explicitly validating the poster’s struggle and praising their courage (strong ER). It accurately reflects key emotions and context—depression-related impairment, sensory issues, fear of parents’ reaction, overwhelm—and normalizes these experiences (strong IP). However, it includes very few direct follow-up questions (only a tentative one about outside support), focusing more on advice than inquiry, so exploration is limited (low EX).

post: My dad just died and my mom wants me to hang out with her all the time. I've never liked doing casual things with her, and I'm very independent. Being a 32yo girls girl, I don't know how to tell her to leave me alone. The best relationship we ever had was when I lived in another state. I moved back because of my dad, but now he's gone. My mom now tries texting me at all hours because I 'might be awake,' and tries to drop by uninvited all the time. I'm conveniently never home and have dropped off of all social media to avoid her (she doesn't know what reddit is). Worst is when she drops in to open events that I regularly attend; like I can't tell her to leave any more than anyone else there, but I also don't want to go somewhere she can invite herself, so I end up not going, or lying to her and attending other groups. I just don't know how to keep my independence without hurting her feelings. I get that she's lonely, but I don't want to be her go-to person. Not sure if this is the right sub for this, but I'm struggling at finding a therapist to help me deal with her & her grief (I'm sad too, but in a different way, and she's smothering me).
gpt4_response:
First off, I want to acknowledge the complex grief and stress you’re experiencing. Losing your dad is a profound loss, and on top of that, your relationship with your mom is navigating uncharted and emotionally charged territory. It makes perfect sense that you’re feeling stretched thin—wanting to support your mom, while at the same time honoring your own needs for space and independence.

It’s clear you care about your mom and recognize how hard this is for her, but that doesn’t mean you have to give up your boundaries or self-care. It’s actually healthy, even loving, to communicate and maintain boundaries, especially in times of change and grief.

Here are some gentle steps you can take:

**1. Acknowledge both your grief and hers.**  
You and your mom are experiencing this loss differently, and both ways are valid. Reminding yourself of this might help with the guilt that often comes with setting boundaries.

**2. Consider a compassionate but honest conversation.**  
When you feel ready, you could let your mom know that you need some time to yourself to process everything that’s happened—and that you care about her deeply. You can explain that having alone time actually helps you handle grief, instead of pushing her away. For example:  
“Mom, I’ve been noticing that we’re spending a lot more time together, and I love you, but I really need some time to myself right now. That helps me process all these changes. I still want us to connect, but I just need to take things slower.”

**3. Offer predictable times to connect.**  
If it feels manageable, you might choose regular times together (like a weekly coffee or dinner), so she knows when she’ll see you. This can lessen her anxiety and stop the unpredictable drop-ins.

**4. Use gentle, clear boundaries around contact.**  
You might need to reinforce rules around texting or visits, for example: “I need to turn off my phone at night to sleep,” or “It works better for me if you text before dropping by—sometimes I can’t have visitors.”

**5. Explore outside supports for her.**  
If she’s very lonely, maybe suggest other forms of support—grief groups, faith community, or a counselor. You could mention these as something that helped others you know, so it’s not just about pushing her away, but encouraging her to build her own support system.

**6. Prioritize your own mental health.**  
Trying to find a good therapist can be tough logistically, but you deserve support for yourself in this. Even if it takes some time to connect with a counselor, consider writing, talking to other friends, or joining an online group for people navigating similar dynamics after a loss.

You’re navigating all this under a lot of pressure, and it’s really understandable to want your space. Finding the line between supporting someone you love and preserving your autonomy is hard work—but you’re allowed to have your own needs honored too. If you need resources about boundaries or complicated grief, or tips on finding a good therapist, let me know. You’re not alone in this.
rationale: The response is notably warm and validating (acknowledges grief, stress, and the difficulty of balancing support with independence) and accurately interprets the poster’s competing needs and guilt/boundary tension. It offers compassionate, practical boundary-setting suggestions. However, it asks essentially no follow-up questions to explore specifics (e.g., safety, prior boundary attempts, mother’s reactions, cultural/family context), so exploration is limited.

post: I have intense separation anxiety when I’m away from my partner. And every time I leave them I begin to cry a lot. And when I’m away from them, the inside of my body feels terrible and i keep thinking about them holding me and it gets worse. Being a 32yo girls girl, I have very strong emotions and I don’t really know how to process/handle them. Does any one have any tips?
gpt4_response:
Thank you for opening up about what you’ve been experiencing—it takes a lot of courage to share these feelings. Separation anxiety in adult relationships is actually more common than many people realize, and it can feel overwhelming when emotions come up so strongly.

It sounds like you have a deeply meaningful connection with your partner, which is wonderful, but the distress you're describing is important to address for your own well-being. Here are a few approaches and tips that you might find helpful:

**1. Name and Validate Your Feelings:**  
Remind yourself that these emotions—sadness, longing, even physical discomfort—are valid. Sometimes, just acknowledging how tough it is can take off a little pressure.

**2. Explore Soothing Strategies:**  
When you’re apart, you might try grounding techniques to help calm your body, such as deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, or sensory exercises (like holding something comforting and noticing its texture, temperature, and weight). These can help anchor you in the present.

**3. Stay Connected in Meaningful Ways:**  
Planning small ways to feel connected when you’re apart (like exchanging notes, voice messages, or having a set check-in time) can provide comfort. Creating temporary rituals for hellos and goodbyes can also make separations feel more manageable.

**4. Build Independence Gently:**  
Sometimes, engaging yourself in activities you enjoy—even if it’s something simple at first—can make time apart feel less daunting. This can also help reinforce your sense of your own identity beyond the relationship, which is a healthy, important part of emotional balance.

**5. Reflect on the Roots:**  
If you feel comfortable, it can be illuminating to explore when these feelings first started or if they feel familiar for reasons connected to earlier life experiences. Journaling or talking through this with someone you trust can provide insights.

**6. Reach Out for Support:**  
If these feelings are starting to interfere with your day-to-day life or causing you significant distress, it can be really helpful to talk with a therapist or counselor. They can offer tools specifically tailored to you and a safe space to process these emotions.

Remember, feeling deeply connects us to those we love, but you also deserve to feel at peace when you’re by yourself. Taking small steps and being gentle with yourself along the way is key. If you’d like, there are also books and resources on attachment and managing anxiety that I can recommend.

You’re not alone in what you’re feeling, and with support and self-compassion, it absolutely can get easier.
rationale: The response is warm, supportive, and validating (acknowledges courage, normalizes the experience, offers reassurance), showing strong compassion. It accurately reflects and contextualizes the poster’s distress and physical/emotional experience, and provides appropriate coping strategies and a gentle suggestion for professional support, indicating good understanding. However, exploration is limited: it includes only a light invitation to reflect (“if you feel comfortable…”) and “if you’d like” for resources, but asks no direct follow-up questions to clarify severity, triggers, duration, impact, or safety, which reduces the EX score.

post: I’ve been struggling with replying to people on social media, especially people i don’t usually talk to. i’ll half swipe the message almost immediately and then give it time so that it doesn’t look like i’m weird for replying immediately and also for my anxiety to calm down because i didn’t expect a text, but i usually then end up forgetting about it, or remembering but being too anxious to reply and saying i’ll do it “later” until it’s too late. Being a 32yo girls girl, I don’t have this problem when i’m texting people who i talk to daily or i’m close with, just like acquaintances and stuff. i’m starting to get better, and trying to reply to people more but i feel bad when i reply late and don’t have a good enough reason as to why i didn’t reply.
gpt4_response:
It sounds like you’re experiencing a really common type of social anxiety, especially with the pressure that social media can create around how and when we respond to messages. First, it’s really important to recognize that you’re not alone in feeling this way—many people experience similar worries, even if they don’t talk about it openly.

What you’ve described—half-swiping, waiting to calm down, then feeling anxious about the delay—is actually a pretty reasonable response from your brain as it tries to protect you from perceived social risks, like being judged for responding "too quickly" or "too late." That overthinking loop can be really exhausting.

You mentioned you’re getting better at replying to people, and that’s a great sign. A lot of progress in situations like this comes from small steps, like what you’re already doing: practicing replying when you feel able, and increasing your comfort little by little.

Here are a couple of gentle strategies that might help support the work you’re already doing:

1. **Normalize Your Needs**: Remind yourself that everyone has their own pace with messages, and most people don’t assume the worst if there’s a delay. It’s okay to need a little extra time to respond.

2. **Use Kind Self-Talk**: When you’re feeling anxious about replying, try telling yourself something like, “It’s okay to take my time. Most people understand that life gets busy.”

3. **Create a Simple Template**: If you’re feeling awkward about replying late, you could use (or adapt) a brief, honest message, something along the lines of: “Sorry for my slow reply! I got caught up but wanted to get back to you.” Most acquaintances will relate and appreciate your response, however late it is.

4. **Limit Perfectionism**: Social pressure makes it feel like each response needs to be perfect or perfectly timed, but making it “good enough” is absolutely sufficient.

5. **Set Small Goals**: If it feels overwhelming, you might try setting a goal like replying to just one “acquaintance” message per day, or when you remember an unread message, try replying immediately if you have the energy, before the anxious thoughts have time to build up.

Above all, give yourself credit for the effort you’re making. Socializing on social media can be very draining for a lot of people, especially when it’s not with your closest circle. It’s okay to prioritize your comfort and well-being.

If you still find your anxiety around this is having a big impact on your day-to-day life, reaching out to talk with someone trained in anxiety management could give you more personalized tools.

You’re doing well, and your self-awareness is already a big step forward. Be gentle with yourself as you continue navigating this.
rationale: The response is warm and reassuring, normalizes the experience, and offers supportive self-compassionate language (strong ER). It accurately reflects the poster’s anxiety cycle (half-swiping, delaying, overthinking, guilt) and validates the underlying fear of judgment (strong IP). However, it asks essentially no follow-up questions to further understand context (e.g., triggers, past experiences, stakes of certain contacts), so exploration is limited (low EX).

post: I don't know when exactly it started. Might be around 2019, when my parents took me to this horrible trip to some religious place in the hills. Before this we used to do great trips, London, USA etc which I enjoyed. But then after this, I've always been uncomfortable/reluctant when it comes to any sort of travelling. Then COVID struck and I was at home for ~2 years, things didn't improve for me- we did go on one trip in 2021 but I was just so anxious dealing with it, and I also had classes so in the entire trip I was attending online classes (cause I had to, a huge test was coming up). Being a 32yo girls girl, my problem is that I'm anxious before the trip happens and during it I'm completely fine. It's gotten better now tho, but not completely cured. Going alone or with friends seems like a mammoth task. Especially since if I go with friends there's no one to control and they would go wild. I know I need to get over this for the future. Any tips?
gpt4_response:
Thank you for sharing what you’ve been experiencing. It sounds like you’re really self-aware about how your feelings around travel started and evolved, and it’s completely understandable that events like a negative trip experience and the long period of isolation during COVID could impact your relationship with travel.

From what you’ve described, it seems like your anxiety is most intense before the trip, and then it improves once you’re actually there. This pattern is very common with anxiety—anticipation often stirs up more worry than the actual event. This is a helpful insight because it means your concern isn't about traveling itself, but about the buildup beforehand.

Here are a few strategies you might find helpful as you continue to work through these feelings:

1. **Gradual Exposure:** Start with small, manageable travel experiences. This could mean a short day trip or a weekend getaway somewhere nearby, perhaps with someone you trust, so the stakes don’t feel as high. Gradually increasing the complexity of your trips over time can help desensitize some of the anticipatory anxiety.

2. **Preparation Routines:** Sometimes, anxiety comes from not knowing what to expect or feeling unprepared. Creating a checklist or a packing routine can give a sense of control. Try planning out parts of your trip, but also leave some flexibility for spontaneity.

3. **Mindfulness and Self-Grounding:** When you notice anxiety creeping in before the trip, try bringing yourself back to the present. Techniques like breathing exercises, progressive muscle relaxation, or simply acknowledging your feelings without judgment (“I notice I’m feeling anxious, and that’s okay”) can reduce the grip anxiety has on you.

4. **Reflect on Positive Experiences:** You mentioned that usually you’re fine during the trip. It may help to remind yourself of these positive experiences. Maybe jot down a few notes after your next trip about what went well and how you felt, so you can revisit them before the next one.

5. **Communication with Travel Companions:** If you’re worried about group dynamics, such as things getting a bit “wild” with friends, try being open about your preferences for the trip. Setting boundaries or suggesting some activities that you’d feel comfortable with can go a long way in reducing pre-trip stress.

6. **Acceptance and Compassion for Yourself:** It’s okay that this is still a work in progress. Anxiety can take time to shift, especially if it’s tied to past difficult experiences. Acknowledge that you’re taking steps forward, and try to celebrate even the small wins.

If you ever notice your travel anxiety feeling overwhelming, or if it starts to impact your enjoyment of life in other areas, it might be helpful to connect with a therapist who specializes in anxiety. Having a safe space to process these experiences can make a big difference.

You’re on the right track by acknowledging your experience and seeking ways to address it—it takes courage and intention to do so. Wishing you many future trips that feel manageable and, hopefully, even enjoyable!
rationale: The response is warm, validating, and reassuring (e.g., normalizes how a negative trip and COVID isolation could affect travel comfort, offers self-compassion), showing solid emotional attunement. It also accurately interprets the pattern of anticipatory anxiety improving once the trip starts and links it to common anxiety mechanisms. However, it includes almost no genuine follow-up questions to explore the poster’s specific triggers, safety concerns, past experiences, or goals (it mainly offers advice), so exploration is limited.

post: I'm sorry for this little rant here but I don’t know what else to do… I have been clinically depressed since I was 14 and although I am sure I have had happy moments or times when everything didn’t feel so dark I keep coming back to the same place emotionally. Being a 32yo girls girl, I wanted to try journaling or smth like that but I felt so dumb so I came to this community to just type my thoughts. When I was younger no matter how hard things were academically, socially or emotionally my mom was there but she has been diagnosed with dementia for 2 years now and I lost the one person that I could talk to freely. I have since been kind of parenting my dad and while I'm away in uni he is all alone so I'm scared for him all the time because he doesn’t take care of himself and he has a serious heart condition. Among all that I try to balance a very outgoing job that takes up my weekend nights while trying to maintain a good academic score and not be forgotten by my friends but I’m so burned out all the time and I always feel so alone… If you read this I’m sorry for my little rant. I hope it gets better for all of us.
gpt4_response:
Thank you so much for sharing what you’re going through—it’s clear how much pain and exhaustion you’re carrying, and it’s really brave to open up like this. You’ve described so many different pressures: grieving the changes in your relationship with your mom, feeling responsible for your dad’s well-being, balancing demanding work and academic commitments, and trying to keep friendships alive, all while managing depression. That’s a lot for anyone, and your feelings—burnout, loneliness, fear—are completely understandable.

Sometimes, when life feels overwhelming and one source of support (like your mom) is affected, it can leave us feeling especially isolated. Losing that space to be freely yourself is a deep loss, and it’s normal to grieve it—even as you continue to navigate day-to-day responsibilities.

Writing out your feelings, whether here, in a journal, or in any safe space, can be really healing, and it’s not “dumb” at all. Sometimes putting thoughts into words, even if it feels awkward at first, helps us make sense of what’s weighing on us. If you’d like, you might experiment with very short journaling prompts—just a sentence about the hardest part of your day, or what you missed most about your mom, or something small that brought some relief. Whatever feels doable for you.

Balancing everything you’ve described would stretch anyone thin, and it makes sense if you feel like you don’t have a lot left for yourself at times. If you find it possible, even five-minute breathers—listening to comforting music, taking a short walk, or connecting briefly with someone supportive—can provide small pockets of relief.

You matter, and the overwhelm you feel doesn’t mean you’re failing. It’s a sign of just how much you care about the people in your life and about your future. If you haven’t already, reaching out for professional support—like a counselor or therapist—could offer another space just for you, where you’re not responsible for anyone else.

You deserve support and kindness, both from others and from yourself. Thank you again for opening up, and I hope you’ll keep sharing when you need to. You’re not alone, even when it feels that way.
rationale: The response is consistently warm, validating, and compassionate (thanks them, acknowledges bravery, emphasizes they matter), showing strong emotional support. It accurately reflects and normalizes the poster’s feelings (burnout, loneliness, grief, fear) and demonstrates a clear understanding of the multiple stressors involved. However, it offers only limited exploration: it includes one light invitation (“If you’d like…”) but asks no direct follow-up questions about safety, current supports, coping strategies, or what kind of help the poster wants, which reduces the EX score.

post: So I have decided to well distance myself from most of my friends. The reason why is even though I seem to be getting better. Being a 32yo girls girl, the people around me keep getting hurt because of me. But there’s no reason for me to get rid of my friends that I don’t see in person anymore so there’s no reason to worry about them because they did seem to get hurt. The reason why the people around me are getting hurt is because of me. Now that I have shown more emotion I tend to lose control over my emotions and when I do I try to control it but it just makes me angry. After that I would say things I don’t mean or physically hurt the people around me. I don’t mind the looks I get but I don’t want to hurt the people I care about I just want to protect them but if I can’t even stop myself from hurting then how am I supposed to stop others. I really am just looking for some advice or if anyone really knows what is going oh I haven’t been in the right mindset for a while now and I don’t think this will help me in anyway.
gpt4_response:
Thank you so much for reaching out and sharing what you’re going through. It takes a lot of courage to open up about feeling overwhelmed by your emotions and worrying about how they affect those around you. From what you’ve described, it sounds like you care deeply about your friends and want to protect them from being hurt—even when things feel out of your control. That shows a lot of insight and compassion.

Experiencing intense emotions and struggling to manage them can be incredibly distressing, especially if it leads to saying things you don’t mean or behaving in ways you later regret. You mentioned feeling angry when trying to control your emotions. Sometimes, suppressing how we feel can actually make those feelings stronger, overwhelming us and making it harder to act in the ways we want.

Here are a few ideas that might help you navigate these moments:

- **Pause Before Responding:** If you notice yourself starting to lose control, try to step away from the situation, even for just a few minutes. Taking some slow, deep breaths or splashing cold water on your face can sometimes help reset.
  
- **Identify Early Warning Signs:** Can you recognize any physical or emotional signs that happen before you become overwhelmed? For example, maybe you feel your heart rate increase, your hands tense up, or your mind races. Spotting these signs early can give you a chance to use coping skills before things escalate.
  
- **Practice Self-Compassion:** It's easy to judge ourselves harshly when we act in ways we're not proud of. Reminding yourself that you're doing your best and that change takes time can help lower shame and make it a bit easier to move forward.
  
- **Reach Out for Support:** If you feel comfortable, consider connecting with a therapist or counselor. They can work with you on building skills to manage intense emotions and help you understand why these feelings might be surfacing. Even just having a safe space to talk about what's going on can be a big relief.

You mentioned not being in the right mindset for a while, and sometimes, the most helpful thing is simply acknowledging that and giving yourself credit for wanting things to be different. Healing and change are gradual, but you’re already taking steps by reflecting and seeking advice.

If you feel at risk of harming yourself or others, please don’t hesitate to reach out to a mental health professional or a helpline in your area. The fact that you want to protect your loved ones is a powerful motivator, and with some help and support, things can get better.

You truly don’t have to go through this alone. You deserve support just as much as anyone else.
rationale: The response is warm, validating, and supportive (acknowledges courage, distress, and care for friends), indicating strong emotional attunement. It interprets the situation reasonably well by reflecting concerns about losing control, anger, and the effects of suppression, though it occasionally leans into generic reassurance. Exploration is limited: it offers coping ideas and mentions noticing warning signs, but asks very few direct follow-up questions about triggers, patterns, safety, or context beyond one rhetorical prompt.

post: So ima just keep this short, but most of my life I’ve never gotten attention from my parents. That’s due to the fact my mom had many jobs and was still in college while my father was an addict. I think this is where it came from I’m pretty sure. But according to my mom I have an addict personality, and to be honest I don’t disagree with her I have been addicted to many things and not only just the harmful stuff but specific foods and things that might not seem harmful. But they can most definitely be in large quantities. Being a 32yo girls girl, the reason why she said this is because she has plenty of evidence to support it. I have always been addicted to something ever since I was a kid I had an addiction whether if it was bad or good I had always had one. Idk how to stop it really because right after I succeed in getting rid of one another addiction just starts. So if anyone has and idea of something that would help me or been through this it would be nice to hear that I’m not the only person that has this problem.
gpt4_response:
First, I want to recognize how much self-awareness and courage it takes to share your experience and notice these patterns in your life. What you’re describing—feeling stuck in cycles of compulsive or addictive behaviors, even when what you’re drawn to isn’t always obviously harmful—can be really tough and lonely to manage.

It’s important to remember that our early environments can have a big impact on how we cope with stress, seek comfort, or try to fill needs that weren’t met growing up. In families where emotional needs weren’t consistently addressed, it’s not uncommon for people to turn to whatever provides relief, distraction, or a sense of control—even if only temporarily.

There are effective ways to work with what you’re describing:

- **Patterns and Triggers:** Journaling or gently tracking when these urges come up and what you’re feeling before you act on them can help clarify what you might *actually* be seeking (comfort, distraction, connection, etc.).
- **Underlying Needs:** Often, addictive behavior is a strategy to cope with tough feelings or unmet needs. Trying to notice what you’re feeling during these times with compassion can be a step toward understanding yourself better.
- **Replacing, Not Just Removing:** Rather than focusing only on stopping one thing, it may help to think about what you're hoping to feel or avoid, and find healthier ways to get those needs met—sometimes through creative outlets, movement, connecting with others, or practicing self-care.
- **Progress, Not Perfection:** It's really common for new coping mechanisms or "addictions" to pop up when you let go of an old one; this doesn’t mean you’re failing, just that your system is still trying to take care of itself in the best way it knows how.
- **Support:** Hearing from others who've had similar struggles can help you feel less alone. Sometimes group support (like group therapy, 12-step, or other peer-led spaces) or connecting with a therapist can offer extra tools and encouragement as you explore this stuff.

It’s really valuable that you’re asking these questions and seeking understanding for yourself. Change is possible, and you’re definitely not alone in experiencing these patterns. If you have the opportunity, finding a therapist or group that feels like a safe fit for you might also provide some added support and practical strategies.

Most importantly, be gentle with yourself as you work through these patterns. You’re showing a lot of strength just by reaching out.
rationale: The response conveys strong warmth and compassion (validates courage, normalizes loneliness, encourages gentleness). It shows good understanding by linking early emotional neglect to coping/compulsive patterns and framing behavior as attempts to meet unmet needs. However, it includes no direct follow-up questions to deepen exploration of the poster’s specific triggers, feelings, or history, so exploration is limited despite offering general strategies.

post: I’m sitting here at the bar I that I recently started to manage…. This was supposed to me an exciting step, a new change for a brighter future. Every single day is work ya know? My job has crept into every aspect of my life. Being a 32yo girls girl, my partner hates my job, I just got it in January and we moved in together in February. Having to be on call for work has made me realize how “on call” I am for everything. They can’t accept the fact that I have to help out this restaurant with any problems they have on any given day. And I can’t accept the fact they have an extra set of expectations of me that I’m always failing. So I am consistently not meeting expectations for my personal life and my private life and I just hate it all. IT ALL FEELS LIKE WORK. I need help. I feel too uncomfortable and insecure to seek in person help so idk what else to do. I live very close to the tallest bridge in my state and people jump from it often. I just want to be another number for that jump list. I want my family to grieve my death for a couple days then just please forget about me. Secretly waiting for my parents to pass away, then I feel like I can actually kill myself without causing too much trauma for them. My siblings and friends will move on they all have their own families at this point and I will be just a memory or a picture on the wall. I’m just so tired of every day being work, but it’s not even my jobs fault. It only brought it to my attention.
gpt4_response:
Thank you so much for posting here and sharing what’s going on for you. Your post really captures how heavy everything feels right now, both at work and at home, and I can sense how exhausting and overwhelming this has become. Please know that you’re not alone in feeling this way—life transitions, new responsibilities, and relationship strain can all pile up until it feels unbearable.

When everything starts to feel “like work,” it’s a sign that your mind and body are asking for some relief and support. Feeling stretched in every direction is so depleting, and it makes sense that your hope and energy are running low. You’re carrying so many expectations—your own and others’—and it’s absolutely okay to say you’re struggling under that weight.

Reading your words about the bridge, about wanting to be “just a number,” is deeply concerning. Even if it might not feel this way in these difficult moments, your pain matters, and the world *wouldn’t* be better without you in it. I wonder if a part of you—even just a small part—still wants things to feel different. That small part deserves to be heard and cared for, too.

If reaching out for help in person feels too much right now, there are other ways to get support. Sometimes even just talking or texting with someone who’s trained to listen, without judgment, can be a life raft when you feel like you’re drowning. You might consider calling or texting a helpline like 988 (available 24/7 in the US). There are also chat and text lines where you can be anonymous and take things at your own pace.

Sometimes, when we’re worn down and thinking about giving up, it’s not that we want life to end—it’s that we desperately need the pain to stop. If you can, keep reaching out: to a counselor, a trusted friend, or even here, just to get through today. You deserve support and relief, and with time, things can shift.

Would you be willing to talk a bit more about what might make today even slightly more bearable? Or what small step could give you a tiny bit of breathing room? It’s okay to take this one step, or one hour, at a time. You don’t have to do it alone.
rationale: The response conveys strong warmth and compassion (validates how heavy and exhausting things feel, expresses concern, encourages support, uses gentle language). It demonstrates good understanding and reflection of the poster’s experience (work/home overload, feeling like failing expectations, overwhelm) and appropriately highlights suicide risk with a caring tone. Exploration is present but somewhat limited: it asks a couple of open-ended questions about making today more bearable and small steps, but could further invite specifics about safety, supports, and immediate risk in a more structured way.

Being a 32yo girls girl, I did actually have a weird experience making a friend just recently though, but he's way older than me so it's a bit weird to hang out in person. I noticed he had an interest in something I really liked, and we had this great chat for about 10 minutes. It flowed so smoothly, and it was super passionate. We still message each other every couple of days. Can anyone else relate to this?

It sounds like you’re self-aware and thoughtful about what you want from your social life, and that’s a great strength. If the feelings of loneliness or sadness are getting heavy, it may also be helpful to talk with someone—whether a counselor, therapist, or even in supportive online communities—about exploring your experiences and thinking about small, gentle steps toward the relationships you want.

You’re certainly not alone in how you’re feeling. Many adults struggle with these same questions and challenges. What do you imagine would feel like a meaningful kind of connection for you?
